data.title: CNYA: Will DeepSeek And Tariffs Alter China's Competitiveness?
data.description:
Investment thesis

It has been over half a year since we last covered BlackRock’s iShares MSCI China A ETF (BATS:CNYA). A lot has happened since then.

Most publicly listed companies in China only report their Q4 and FY results in March. Therefore, we do not have fresh data on their earnings. By extrapolating what we saw in Q3, we expect an average earnings growth to be in line with the 5% growth to the country’s GDP.

In this article, we want to focus on key items that might have the largest impact on the development of the stock market in China.

First, let us start by looking at how the ETF CNYA has performed.

Performance of CNYA

The share price of CNYA did grow by a respectable 14.5% over the last 12 months.

CNYA and SPY should be seen as barometers for the stock markets of the world's two largest economies. The gap between the Chinese and the American market is not that large over one year. However, when we compare the two over five years, the S&P 500 is up 81% and CNY is up only 7%.

Here are topics we think are important to the development of China's economy and the companies' share prices.

DeepSeek

One reason the American “Magnificent Seven” did so well last year, was all the well-deserved attention they got around AI. It spilled into the whole market.

It will certainly change many ways in which we live and work. This development was led by innovation in the U.S.

When we wrote the conclusion to our previous article on CNYA, we did not know then that the development of AI would come from China this quickly. We were mostly thinking of all the benefits it will have on their industries.

We are no expert on this matter. However, in an article in SCMP in Hong Kong, titled “How AI start-up DeepSeek upended industry assumptions and changed China’s narrative”, we learned that DeepSeek’s release of reasoning model and large language model offers rivaling performance to OpenAI’s offerings at a fraction of the cost with similar quality.

The important lesson from what is taking place is to not underestimate China’s ability to innovate and bring to market technologies and products that are, to quote a Singapore minister;

“Cheaper, Faster, and Better”

Manufacturing and export

On the topic of faster, never have we seen so fast action from any government, as what we are now seeking coming out of the U.S.

As of writing, the U.S. has imposed a tariff of 25% on the import of all goods from Canada and Mexico. With China, it is a 15% import tax. In addition, they have also stated that there will be an import tax of 25% on steel and aluminum from Mexico, Canada, and the EU. These are to come into effect from the 12th of March.

These are supposedly based on reciprocity. On the other hand, it was said the tariffs are there to stop fentanyl coming into the U.S. through these countries. Or maybe it is both. It is hard to tell.

Europe will also be hit.

China has hit back with a 15% import tax on LNG and coal and 10% on crude oil. Exactly where these tariffs will end might change, as they are also seen as a negotiating tool.

We do not think the tariffs on Chinese-made goods will reduce the volume. Manufacturers in China are already selling goods with low-profit margins. In our opinion, it will be the consumers in the U.S. and Europe, which will have to bear most of the increase in the sale prices.

In our last article, we covered the influx of Chinese-made EVs flooding the global market. It was particularly in the EU, which does have a large automotive industry, where this became a concern. They raised tariffs of up to 37.6% effective from the 5th of July 2024.

The latest number from the General Administration of Customs in China, we can see that the drop in the export of EVs has been considerable.

It was our belief last summer that these setbacks to China’s export of EVs would be temporary. We still hold the same view. However, we would like to add some context to that statement.

Companies like BYD Company Ltd. (OTCPK:BYDDF) (OTCPK:BYDDY), which is now one of the top 10 holdings of CNYA, is still a “rookie”, when you compare it against century-old companies like Ford Motor Company (F) and Toyota Motor (TM). It will continue to grow, both domestically in China, and on a global scale. Temporary setbacks are inevitable.

According to a publication from the State Council of PRC, total exports in 2024 from China amounted to RMB 25.45 trillion, a growth of 7.1% Y-o-Y. Import also grew by 2.3%, expanding to RMB 18.39 trillion.

The property market

The property sector used to account for 25% of the nation’s GDP. It has been important on so many levels.

The first level is each provincial government has relied on the sale of leases of land as their largest income provider. Secondly, the real estate developer's cost of building homes. With that comes myriads of middlemen involved in the transactions of properties, including the mortgagors.

Then you have the effect of the wealth creation, when it did happen, which trickled down to the economy through positive consumer sentiment.

According to data from the National Bureau of Statistics, as of last year, total new residential sales had halved since reaching a peak in 2021, dropping to RMB 9.7 trillion. There is still a large inventory of unsold residential properties from developers, but analysts in China do see some improvements in sales prices and volumes and believe a recovery will start in 2025.

Affordability is always an issue, and we therefore move on to the issue of the cost of capital.

The economy

According to the World Bank’s latest estimate at the end of last, China’s GDP grew by 4.9%, and they have projected it to come in slightly lower at 4.5% this year.

When we last analyzed CNYA, the 5-year rate for property mortgages was 3.95%. It is now 3.6%.

Contrary to most other OECD countries, China has not had a problem with inflation over the last ten years. The highest it went was in 2018 when it reached 2.9%. Last year, it was only 0.2%. The National Bureau of Statistics of China forecast for this year is an inflation of about 1.6%.

The urban unemployment rate in China is 5.1%.

However, youth unemployment has received quite a lot of attention. Two years ago, it was reported as 20%, which prompted the government to stop publishing it. They have now revised the definition of unemployment to exclude students, and since it covers the age group of 16-24, a large portion of this age group is studying. It has been falling gradually over the last few months, and according to SCMP, as of November of last year, it stood at 16.1%. It would be more of a concern if it was high for the age group 25-29 years of age, but this is 6.7%, only slightly higher than the total unemployment rate.

Risks and conclusion

We cannot rule out global economic slowdowns. Although, it does not seem to be very likely for 2025. The economy in the U.S. is strong. Europe will muddle through, as usual.

However, investing in China by foreign investors could still be muted.

If we revisit the fundamentals that we shared in July last year, we note that the valuations are somewhat higher. However, the gap between the two remains the same.

We are not too concerned about the tariffs from the U.S. and EU.

Chinese steel, which is the third-largest exporter to the U.S. could grow on the back of overcapacity in China and their competitive advantage. How long that would last is dependent on the mood in the White House and its relationship with China.

Uncertainties are now the “new normal”.

Nevertheless, we look beyond the usual presidential cycles that take place every four years in the U.S. Over time, China will most likely become less reliant on just two trading blocks.

Trade with countries participating in the Belt and Road Initiative now accounted for 50% of all of China’s trade in 2024. This will most likely continue to grow.

China will remain competitive and offer good value.

Based on valuation, we keep our Buy call for CNYA.

data.title: Brad Simpson: Challenging The Narrative Around Tariffs And Their Impact On Markets
data.description:
With current U.S. trade policy creating an atmosphere of uncertainty for investors, Brad Simpson, Chief Wealth Strategist at TD Wealth, discusses strategies to help find clarity in the markets and position your portfolio to mitigate risk.

Transcript

Kim Parlee: Concerns about trade have been weighing on global markets as investors consider the potential economic impact on corporate earnings. Well, my next guest is here to challenge the narrative of that impact and what it could mean for markets. Brad Simpson is Chief Wealth Strategist at TD Wealth, and he joins me now. Nice to have you here.

Brad Simpson: It's great to be here.

Kim Parlee: Let's start with talking a bit about the narrative that has been developing around trade. We hear about, of course, tariffs have been-- are going to be implemented on steel and aluminum-- and we're hearing about more could be coming. And the impact on the markets, though-- that is really what you're here to talk about. So tell me what-- tell me what we mean when people are talking about and a bit about what we actually see happening.

Brad Simpson: Ah, you know, I'd go as far as to say this is-- in 34 years, this is one of the most remarkable times in my whole career. And I would say it comes down to-- if you think about, especially if you took the period from February 1 to the 3-- it's a Saturday to the Monday when we were when we were first talking about the first tariffs. And the long story short is if I looked at those three days... the Saturday, I read about tariffs all day. Sunday, I had two conference calls. Sunday-- two conference calls. Read about it all day. Sunday morning-- or Monday morning, I got up at 3:45 in the morning. I did three conference calls about this. So it was about three or four hours in. I had two meetings. So before it was even 10:00, I was five hours into talking tariffs.

And the long story short is, the middle of the market being open and looking at it and looking on FactSet and looking on my Bloomberg terminal and looking at all the noise around it. And then checking out and seeing what others were saying and all the hyperbole around it. And the reality is that markets were very calm. And there was something missing. Like somehow in the middle of all this noise and what was going on in the market, and even like reading about what happened in the market that day afterwards and how it's been-- the reality is, is markets have been very calm. And that-- and so I wanted to frame that. So I brought a chart along to kind of try to look at it.

So the one on the left, basically what it's saying is-- I took February 1 and looked at the change of the market during the day. So really, there was a high-low movement of the S&P 500 between 75-- down 75 to 100 to 150 basis points.

And all in all, if you kind of look at the market going back to 2000 and think of how many things have happened since 2000 in the markets, markets have traded like they did on February 1st 10% of the time, meaning that this was not an extraordinary day in any way. It was hectic, but it was an ordinary day in markets.

The one on the right, what I'm showing is, is going back and looking at when the first tariffs were implemented between the United States and China in 2018. And markets were moving from zero to down 4% for about three months there. That's high volatility.

And what I'm trying to-- what I wanted to frame with this is to say that every year we have our national conference and our research conference, which is on January 20. And I put this slide up on the screen, and I said, is the pricing mechanism of the market broken?

And so when non-finance guys speak, we say that going out 6 to 12 months is the market's pricing what you think is going to happen. And what I'm saying, was it broken? Was I was saying that who doesn't know that there's going to be tariffs? And the reality is, that the market knows this.

And so, what's happened from 2018 to now is that the market's become less extreme. The noise around it has become more extreme. And there's an amazing dichotomy in that. And I think that to continue to build the frame this way is just to understand that really, that the pricing mechanism of the market is working very well.

I think that for investors, the best thing to do to start with is that-- almost everybody's become an expert in tariffs now.

Kim Parlee: Yeah. In the same way it happened with COVID, the same way it happened with everything before it. Yeah.

Brad Simpson: And my suggestion is to start out-- let's just use this as a base case. There's going to be tariffs, full stop. And if we use that as your base case, then it gives you a lot of understanding, is that every time you hear a different thing happen and different change, is to go, well no, wait a minute. I was expecting this to happen, and so is the market.

And then, that really turns the volume down, and it gets it back to really what our job is to do. And our job to do is to manage capital and allocate capital and grow and protect it. And the reality is, is there's lots of good things going on in the economy where we can do that.

Kim Parlee: Yeah. I was going to say it's fascinating to hear that, too. Because yeah, when you think about the advent of social media, everything is amplified. And so to see some facts to show that that's actually not the case in the markets is coming. So thank you. That's actually nice to see.

Let's talk a bit about, you know-- the bottom line is when we want to go from an asset allocation standpoint, maybe let's talk about what you think about in terms of the different assets. So, I don't know-- if we want to start with fixed income, and you can tell us what you're thinking on that front.

Brad Simpson: Sure. Well, let's-- even for that, if we took a step to go, how are we going to make those decisions? And so, our starting point is that ultimately when you're making decisions, you have to determine where you think the economy is going to go.

Because if you look over the long term, if you look over 15 years, what makes investment works is over 15 years it's almost impossible to lose money. And the reason for that is there's usually a couple economic cycles during that time. And so, the starting point is for us is to say, if we can figure out where we are in the economy, if you can come to terms with the first and say that there will be tariffs.

And then you can-- the second one, you go, I'm going to determine what's happening in the major industrialized nations, the ones with all the skin in the game. So what's happening in Canada? What's happening in the United States? What's happening in the eurozone? What's happening in China?

And if you look at it in those terms and say, if I can frame that. And so we actually come up with just one consistent framing to say, let's look at employment. Let's look at manufacturing. Let's look at what the service economy is doing. Let's look at what--

Kim Parlee: Taxation.

Brad Simpson: There's that. And then let's look at monetary policy, and how much liquidity and what interest rate levels are. And if we're looking at that consistently, and we're looking how that's changing and how that's evolving in a world that where we know that there is this ongoing change in the tariff, we can start having a pretty good range in each of those major trading nations. And really, those are the nations that make the world's market go around, the world's economy go around.

And if you can do that-- and for us it's-- and that's our approach to doing things, I think if you do it, then you start to come into the conclusions how you're going to allocate. And again, if you think about it, everything I'm saying here is coming down to, I want to cut out the noise.

Kim Parlee: Right.

Brad Simpson: Right? I want to-- remember, I always say that it's not what you think, it's how you think. And for us, we're just consistently going and coming back and looking at things in a consistent way so we can take that noise out and allocate it.

Kim Parlee: When we think about asset allocation, maybe take us through the different asset classes and what you're thinking. Fixed income right now is a modest underweight. Why?

Brad Simpson: Well, when you're looking at a concern that there could be an escalation of inflation again-- just a concern, not making a call there-- then really, what you're doing is you're investing for the coupon, for the interest rate return. We don't really think there's going to be a lot of interest rate volatility. So there's maybe some opportunity for capital gains. But for the most part, clip the coupon on your fixed income. It's a good coupon and still makes an awful lot of sense.

Kim Parlee: Let's get into equities, because I know you have a little bit more behind here. And I think-- and correct me-- it's modest overweight on equities. Take us through your thinking.

Brad Simpson: And I think I-- well, I know I brought a chart along for this, and it kind of hits at home with this, is that I think we all know that how much over the last few years that returns and equity markets were derived by technology.

And so, when we look at the economy today, we make this pronouncement. And we say, we're not talking about a soft landing anymore. If anything, we're not even talking about an early-stage economy. This economy has been moving so fast, we have an economy that we think is mid-stage. And so, if it's a mid-stage economy-- which we think it is-- we think it's a healthy one.

And in an environment like this is, one, what we're showing on the left here is saying that cap-weighted versus the equal-weighted-- the equal weight is those other 493 names. And lo and behold, we think there's lots of opportunity there. 14% to 16% are earnings growth expectations across sectors, and a very, very healthy mid-stage economy, typically when an equal-weight index does quite well.

The right, what we're looking at is showing manufacturing picking up. As manufacturing is picking up, what tends to do well is, small- and mid-cap companies tend to do well in an environment like that.

And what we're showing on the last one is looking at there so much so, saying that if we look at the earnings expectation of small-cap companies and mid-cap companies, they are actually much higher than our expectations for large-caps, which is the complete opposite of what we saw in 2022 and '23 and '24.

And so, with that, we think that the fears and concerns around concentrated equity markets, they're understandable. The reality is this is a market that's really broadening out. And we think it presents an awful lot of opportunity for investors.

Kim Parlee: You have another chart here I want to bring up, too, and tell me a bit about this one from an equity standpoint, why this is material.

Brad Simpson: Yeah. It's actually-- it's twofold. What we're looking at here is, is we're looking at the correlation between equities and stocks. So, how are they moving together? When you build a diversified--

Kim Parlee: And these are fixed incomes.

Brad Simpson: Actually, fixed. Excuse me. Yeah, yeah. So, long story short is you want your fixed income and equities to move in opposite directions. What we're showing here is that they're increasingly moving in the same direction. And so, as a diversifier, they're not as reliable as they used to be.

And so for us, is to say is really what our notion here is, is that we are an asset allocation team that believes an awful lot in building portfolios with different diversifiers. And so, what we're trying to say is this is an environment where, if you're concerned about fixed income not being as good of a diversifier-- which this is showing that-- you want to get that from other areas. You want to hedge. You want to use long, short, and market-neutral strategies.

And in an environment-- if you look at the equity environment right now, what I'm showing you is as more and more names are moving out, that creates more and more opportunities for you to go long some of those names, short some of the others. That's a very good diversifier. And I would say, like, where we are with concerns about volatility in the marketplace, very much a changing economic environment, this is a very good strategy and way to build a better diversified...

Kim Parlee: And an active strategy.

Brad Simpson: Yeah. And so this also shows that active management, even long only, is a good environment for that.

Kim Parlee: I've only got about a minute here, and I apologize, because alternatives-- you have a modest overweight; commodities-- modest underweight. And I'm just thinking, again, this is when you think about the diversification as well, too.

Brad Simpson: Totally. So, one, we like infrastructure. I think, look, we have an awful lot of buildout in infrastructure. I mean, if you look at anything going on in the world, you'll see that--

Kim Parlee: Data centers.

Brad Simpson: --your core plus, your data centers, for sure. But even like your electricity grid, utilities, all those pieces. And so, we like that. We like that an awful lot.

And commodities-- I mean, if you look at the uncertainty that we had in December, where both fixed income and equity markets were under a little bit of pressure, commodities were up. And in an uncertain geopolitical environment, commodities are an excellent diversifier in portfolios. And with the supercycle that we like as well, we think we continue to like those in portfolios as well.

Kim Parlee: 15 seconds. I'm looking at the note that I know you're about to put out. So, I will say, you have here how to not get lost. Tell me a bit about that.

Brad Simpson: Well, I think it comes really full circle for us is that-- and as we talked about-- in this world of such extreme opinions and such extreme positions, that I think it's imperative that you have a process for how you think, that you have a diversified portfolio. But a diversified portfolio that I think it goes well beyond just owning stocks and bonds, making sure that if you're using hedging strategies and using things like private equity and private debt, private credit, investments like that. Be consistent with it. Try to keep out a lot of that noise. This is a lot-- this is a much better environment than what you think.

Original Post

data.title: NNN REIT: Collecting Almost 6% From Commercial Real Estate Without The Headaches
data.description:
There is a lot of information out there glorifying investing in physical real estate, whether you're surfing the web or watching YouTube videos. Real estate can be an incredible investment for some as you can build wealth through sweat equity, leverage, and perseverance, but it's not a risk-free journey. Many people, including myself, have seen home prices increase over the years, as it seems like real estate prices continue to go higher. While the idea of finding a great rental property and only needing to put a fraction of the cost down to acquire the investment is alluring, I know that I am not built for the commitment.

Outside of my personal real estate, I have no interest in being a landlord, dealing with tenants, or managing the issues that arise from being a landlord. This is why I look for real estate investment trusts ("REIT") that specialize in triple net leases, as these are my favorite types of agreements between landlords and tenants. NNN REIT (NYSE:NNN) has 3,568 properties throughout its portfolio with over 98% occupancy rates, and most of the leases are tied to a triple net structure. Instead of having to deal with the headaches that I don't have the time for, I would rather build out a portfolio of REITs that own physical properties and collect the passive income. NNN has been relatively flat over the past year and, more recently, has declined by -18.42% since reaching $49.57 in October. The decline in share price has pushed the dividend yield close to 6%, and I think there is value that needs to be unlocked. I am bullish on NNN as I feel the commercial real estate market will get stronger as 2025 progresses due to upcoming rate cuts and that its shares will appreciate.

Following up on my previous article about NNN REIT

The last time I wrote an article about NNN was last January (can be read here), and since then, shares have declined by 3.69%. When the dividend is factored in, NNN's total return is +3.06% compared to the S&P 500, which climbed 27.49%. I had discussed why I felt NNN was undervalued, and for the majority of 2024, I was correct until I wasn't. Shares have been declining for several months, and they finally look like they have leveled off. I am following up with a new article because a lot has changed over the past year, including a Fed Pivot on rates and a new administration coming into office. I think that some of the REITs that focus on physical real estate are undervalued and that NNN is in a strong position to outperform in 2025. After going through their 2024 fiscal year, I wanted to discuss why I think the sell-off was overdone and outline why I am adding to my position.

Risks to investing in NNN REIT

While NNN has amassed a large portfolio of assets, there are still significant risks to investing in NNN. Just because I am bullish on NNN doesn't mean that the investment will work out how I envision it. Real estate is sensitive to interest rates and macroeconomic factors. If we experience an economy that is too hot, the Fed could reverse its stance on rate cuts and start to take rates higher. This will increase the cost of capital, and NNN's borrowing costs could increase. If we experience an economic slowdown or a large increase in e-commerce, it could lead to some of NNN's tenants downsizing their physical brick-and-mortar footprint. This would negatively impact NNN's occupancy rates and likely cause a decline in revenue and profitability. Depending on how bad a slowdown gets, it could cause disruptions in capital markets and affect their tenant's business models, which could lead to a default on leases. If NNN has properties that are located in areas where there is a shift in population trends, it may not be worth holding on to the property, and a disposition may cause a loss of capital as NNN may have to sell for unfavorable terms.

There are also other large-scale REITs, such as Realty Income (O), and if NNN's properties aren't in premier locations, they could lose out on new business or lose current clients when their leases are up. I happen to believe that NNN is undervalued and that shares will provide capital appreciation and dividend income, but I could always be wrong, and investors should do their own due diligence before allocating capital toward this investment.

NNN is delivering on its key performance indicators, and I think the selloff is an opportunity

When I look at NNN's income statement, I am pleased with the trajectory I am seeing. On a YoY basis, NNN has increased its revenue by $41.2 million (4.98%) and generated an additional $36.8 million (4.87%) in EBITDA and $25.80 million (4.99%) in operating income. Despite a pandemic and a real estate scare during an inflationary environment, NNN has grown its revenue, EBITDA, and operating income every year since 2020. When I look at the long-term trajectory of NNN's revenue and profitability, I see that it's all trending up and to the right. Over the past 9 years, NNN has increased its annualized revenue by $386.40 million (80.02%). This has allowed NNN to generate an additional $368.60 million (87.04%) in EBITDA and $251.20 million (86.12%) in operating income. These are some of the most critical metrics because it indicates that management is able to drive value for shareholders and provide enough income to maintain its annualized dividend increases.

There is a lot to like about the way NNN has structured its portfolio. NNN has 3,568 properties across the country with more than 385 tenants, which adds a tremendous amount of diversification to its revenue stream. NNN focuses on single-tenant triple net lease properties such as a Wendy's or 7-11. The triple net lease structure is my favorite when it comes to REITs because the tenant is responsible for everything that has to do with the property, from repairs to property taxes. NNN has an occupancy rate of 98.3%, and over the past two decades, the lowest its occupancy rate got throughout the financial crisis and the pandemic was 96.4%. NNN's lease structure falls between 10-20 years, and there is currently a 9.9-year weighted average on the remaining lease terms. Only 7.4% of its leases are terming by the end of 2026, while more than half of them expire after 2033. NNN has tied either a fixed rate increase or one that is comparable to CPI on an annual basis, which has helped grow its revenue over the years.

At the end of 2024, NNN had $1.2 billion of availability on its bank credit line, with $10.8 billion in total assets. The retail net lease strategy generates a reliable income stream with low volatility, which has allowed NNN to amass a large portfolio with room for acquisitions if they are viable. This has allowed NNN to pay a strong dividend that has increased annually over the past 34 years. Currently, NNN is paying a dividend of $2.32, which is a 5.74% yield. NNN has grown the dividend at an average rate of 2.42% over the past five years. I believe that its portfolio of assets and triple net lease structure will allow NNN to continue increasing its dividends in the foreseeable future. This is certainly my preferred way to generate income from real estate, as I can sit back and collect the income and then reinvest the dividends without having to deal with any problems.

NNN REIT looks undervalued compared to its peers

I always compare REITs to their peers to see how their dividend income and valuation stacks up against other companies I can invest in. I normally compare NNN to Realty Income, Agree Realty Corporation (ADC), Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT), Simon Property Group (SPG), and Kimco Realty Corporation (KIM). I look at the multiple I am paying for their funds from operations (FFO) and EBITDA, while looking at the dividend yield and its coverage ratio.

Currently, NNN trades at the lowest valuation on a price to FFO and market cap to EBITDA methodology. The peer group trades at an average price to FFO of 14.26x, and NNN trades at 12x its FFO. On a market cap to EBITDA methodology, it's a similar story as the peer group trades at an average of 12.66x its EBITDA while NNN trades at 9.57x its EBITDA. This is a clear indication to me that NNN is undervalued, especially since they have increased their revenue, EBITDA, and operating income on an annual basis since 2020.

REITs are income investments for me, so I am always trying to pay the best multiple for a company's profitability while getting the most bang for the buck in dividend yield. NNN has a dividend yield of 5.74%, which is the second-largest in the peer group and well above the peer group average of 4.86%. NNN's dividend yield is also well covered by its FFO, as the coverage ratio is 1.45x. I think that NNN dividend is very appealing as it exceeds the risk-free rate of return with more than three decades of growth and a strong coverage ratio.

Conclusion

Shares of NNN have declined by more than -15% since reaching their 52-week highs back in October, which has pushed the dividend yield to almost 6%. NNN has an occupancy rate that exceeds 98%, and its revenue and EBITDA continue to increase YoY. I think that the real estate market is going to recover quite well over the next several years as the Fed reduces rates to a more neutral rate. NNN is trading at a low price to FFO and market cap to EBITDA ratio compared to its peers, and I believe that there is value to be unlocked in its current share price. I am very bullish on NNN and believe this is a strong candidate for both capital appreciation and dividend income over the next year.

data.title: Why I'm Still Bullish Despite PayPal's Challenges
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last covered PayPal (NASDAQ:PYPL) in November 2024 with a strong buy rating and technical price target of $87. The stock hit the average price target as expected but missed the optimistic level ($96) by ~$3. The coverage was focused on transaction surge and earnings growth in Q3 '24. Recently, the company released Q4 '24 earnings. The current analysis goes deep into the shifts in operational metrics, valuations, and the state of the ongoing thesis. There is also an observation of downsides attached to branded checkout growth amid sector-specific dependence. Despite this, PayPal remains deeply undervalued, still trading at ~15x forward earnings well below historical averages. I remain bullish, driven by a 60% YoY surge in free cash flow to $6.77B and an aggressive $15B buyback, accounting for ~19% of its market cap.

PayPal’s Growth Balancing Act: Strong TPV, Rising Users, but Branded Checkout Faces Pressure

PayPal had steady growth both in terms of TPV and top-line during CY24. Its TPV grew by 10% YoY, reaching an approximate $1.7T. The growth indicates PayPal is processing an immense number of transactions and PayPal is dominating across markets. The TPV growth by 10% YoY is a crucial figure, especially due to competition across digital payments. The growth comes from the capacity to add active users as well as grow across categories of merchants. The top-line growth has an upward momentum, achieving a YoY growth rate of 7% to $32B. The YoY growth rate of 7% across the top line, while less than TPV, indicates PayPal is tightly monetizing the payment volume. The growth indicates PayPal scaling the transactions’ volume while maintaining the take rate (which is an excellent indicator).

However, PayPal’s TPV expansion slowed down to 7% YoY in Q4 2024, down from Q3’s 9% and Q2’s 11%. The slowdown arises from shifting toward unbranded, low-margin, high-volume processing (Braintree), which has grown to 36% of TPV, while the higher-margin Branded Checkout dipped to 28%, down from 33% in 2021. Unbranded processing slowed down to only 2% expansion in Q4 versus Q3’s 11%, indicating PayPal’s trade-off between profitability versus volume. In the same way, Venmo TPV expanded by 10%, driven by debit card and Pay with Venmo. PayPal, however, loses checkout share to Apple Pay, Google Pay, and Shopify. PayPal’s volume has turned significantly merchant-driven versus consumer-driven, as mid-single-digit TPV expansion is expected in 2025. The future challenge lies in revitalizing Branded Checkout; otherwise, TPV expansion will continue slowly, as declining margins restrict the upside.

We have previously analyzed why PayPal’s product mix shift is changing strategically to unbranded processing (now 36% vs 35% in 2023). The company leveraged Braintree to maintain relevance among merchants while its consumer-facing dominance weakened. However, as seen in Q4’s slowdown in unbranded processing growth, PayPal is now trying to rebalance profitability, signaling that this shift—while not entirely desperate—was a necessary adaptation to defend its market position. The real question is whether PayPal can reinvigorate Branded Checkout and avoid becoming just another low-margin payment processor.

With the growth in TPV and top-line, there is an increase in transaction margin dollars (+5% YoY excluding the benefit of interest on customer balances). So, the growth in transaction margin dollars indicates that PayPal is increasing its transaction volume and improving its profitability per transaction. In terms of scaling, PayPal’s total active accounts returned to growth in CY24 with a significant turnaround. There was an increase of ~9M active accounts YoY, and hit a total of 434M active accounts. This growth in active accounts points to PayPal’s capability to attract new users and re-engage dormant ones.

Moreover, monthly active accounts (MAAs) made steady progress and increased by 2% YoY to 229 million. The growth in MAAs is a positive sign of user engagement. It indicates that more users are actively using PayPal’s services on a regular basis. The increase in MAAs is based on contributions from both PayPal consumer accounts and Venmo, PayPal’s peer-to-peer payment platform. Venmo derived high user engagement. Its engaged user base is growing by 4% in Q4 '24 to hit >64 million monthly active accounts.

The platform’s (Venmo) monetization efforts are also showing positive leads. Here, monetized Venmo monthly active accounts beyond P2P and instant transfers are growing >20% in Q4 '24. This growth is based on the adoption of the Venmo debit card and Pay with Venmo. These have seen monthly active users grow by >30% and 20%. In short, the company has made high investments in marketing and go-to-market strategies. These have started to yield results.

Venmo’s expansion beyond categories, coupled with partnerships with well-known names like Instacart and MoonPay, has fueled it. An example includes the first airline, JetBlue, introducing Venmo as an accepted form of flight bookings. It is a significant milestone for the platform. These partnerships increase the platform’s acceptance and derive higher transaction volumes. While Venmo is still early in its monetization level, the platform’s proven playbook and growing user engagement may provide further top-line growth in CY25 and beyond.

Lastly, PayPal’s moves to accelerate growth for small and medium-sized businesses (SMBS) are also paying off. The company has moved from offering a disparate set of payment products to building an end-to-end suite of solutions that address the payment and transaction needs of SMBs. PayPal Complete Payments now accounts for 45% of SMB processing and checkout volume. Merchants on this platform benefit from PayPal’s upgraded branded checkout experiences. It derives higher conversion rates and improves the overall experience.

PayPal’s merchant financing solutions (like PayPal Working Capital and PayPal Business Loan) are also leading to the company’s top-line growth with SMBs. These financing products increase engagement, pushing the PayPal flywheel. Merchants typically increase their PayPal volume by 36% after adopting PayPal Working Capital and 16% after taking a PayPal Business Loan. In CY24, PayPal’s merchant lending originations reached $3B. All these elements may continue to push the company’s top-line growth in the upcoming quarters.

Issues In Branded Checkout Growth Amid Sector-Specific Dependence

PayPal’s branded checkout volume growth in the US has shown some signs of improvement, but the company faces issues in maintaining and accelerating this growth. In Q4 '24, PayPal had a sequential improvement in US branded checkout volume, with a three-point growth QoQ. However, this growth was largely based on specific vertical exposures like travel, crypto, and gamin rather than a broad-based improvement in market share. This suggests that PayPal’s branded checkout growth is still heavily dependent on specific sectors. This may not be sustainable if these sectors face major downturns.

Internationally, PayPal’s branded checkout volume growth has shown softness (particularly in Europe). While the company continues to take market share internationally, the less than one-point pullback in international branded checkout volume indicates that PayPal is facing issues. This is concerning because international markets represent a significant portion of PayPal’s total payment volume (TPV). So, any slowdown in these regions could hinder overall growth.

For CY25, PayPal expects branded checkout TPV to grow at a mid-single-digit rate against the market’s 22% annual TPV growth. This is in line with the growth seen in CY24. While this is an optimistic signal, PayPal does not anticipate a faster rate of brand-checkout expansion during the launch of new innovations like Fastlane and streamlined checkout processes. The company’s conservative forecast may signal issues in achieving traction over very aggressive digital payments competition from the likes of Apple Pay, Google Pay, and Shopify.

PayPal’s unbranded volume growth has decelerated under the company’s strategy to prioritize profitability over volume. This strategy focuses on renegotiating agreements with large merchants to reflect the value PayPal provides. However, it has led to a slowdown in growth in unbranded processing volume. In Q4 '24, PayPal’s unbranded processing volume grew by only 2% against 11% in Q3 '24. This deceleration may continue in CY25. Why? The company is anticipating a five-point headwind to top-line growth due to ongoing negotiations with large Braintree merchants. PayPal expects the renegotiations to be accretive to transaction margin dollars by one point in CY25. But the trade-off is a high drag on top-line growth.

Finally, the company emphasizes value-added services, including FX-as-a-Service and Risk-as-a-Service. It is intended to offset some of the revenue headwinds from unbranded volume deceleration. However, these services are still in the early stages of adoption, and the impact on the overall bottom line is still not certain. In short, PayPal’s capability to monetize these services sharply can reduce the negative effects of slower unbranded volume growth, but the company’s guidance suggests that it may take time for these initiatives to hit traction. These top-line issues may keep the PS ratio near the long-term minimum and cap the upside in the stock price.

Takeaway

PayPal’s TPV growth remains solid, but a shift toward lower-margin unbranded processing and competition in branded checkout present challenges. While Venmo monetization and merchant financing are gaining traction, the company must reignite branded checkout growth to sustain long-term profitability. Despite these challenges, strong free cash flow growth and an aggressive $15B buyback signal shareholder value creation.

data.title: Karman: Shooting Higher After Its IPO
data.description:
Shares of Karman Holdings (NYSE:KRMN) have skyrocketed higher after its initial public offering. Following a strong pricing process, shares rose by nearly 50% after its public offering, as investors like the positioning of this missile, defense & space business.

While the business is very profitable, and is showing impressive growth, the issue is that of valuations, as these have run quite high to about 13 times sales and a very elevated earnings multiple. Given these valuations, this remains a show-me-first story, certainly after the optimism priced in soon after the public offering here.

That said, the interesting positioning warrants continued interest in the firm, as I look forward to the upcoming results and 2025 outlook in the upcoming earnings release, but for now, I am not going along the ride here.

Missile, Defense & Space

Karman focuses on the design, testing, manufacturing and sale of critical systems for existing and emerging missile, defense and space programs. The company in particular derives revenues from integrated payload protection, propulsion and interstage system solutions, applied in a wide range of current and new programs.

The company claims that it derives revenues from no less than 100 programs within space, missile, hypersonic and defense applications, from about 70 different customers. The company nonetheless relies on a small customer group for about two-thirds of sales, including many governmental clients.

The company is split pretty evenly between three market types: including missiles and IDS, hypersonic and SMD and Space & Launch. Propulsion Systems, as a product family, makes up nearly half of sales. This is complemented by payload protection (responsible for about a third of sales), with the remainder of revenues generated from aerodynamic interstage.

In these markets, the company is working with and competing against firms like Lockheed Martin (LMT), SpaceX, Boeing (BA) and Northrop Grumman (NOC), to name a few.

Valuation & IPO Thoughts

Karman aimed to sell 21.1 million shares at a preliminary price between $18 and $20 per share, with final pricing set at $22 per share. With 8.4 million shares sold by the company itself, the company raised gross proceeds of $185 million.

With a total share count of approximately 132 million shares, the company was awarded a $2.9 billion equity valuation at the offer price. This valuation excludes a $428 million net debt load as of the third quarter of 2024, as net debt is seen closer to $250 million if we factor in net cash proceeds from the public offering. This grants the company a $3.15 billion enterprise valuation at the offer price.

The company has seen spectacular growth in recent years. In the year 2022, the company generated $226 million in sales, on which it reported operating profits of $20 million. Revenues rose by nearly 24% to more than $280 million in 2023 as operating profits more than doubled to $48 million.

The pace of growth was maintained in the first three quarters of 2024. Revenues in the first nine months of 2024 rose by 24% to $254 million, on which solid operating profits were reported at $49 million. Growth is set to be maintained in the fourth quarter, with full year sales seen up 24% to $346 million, as near 20% operating profit margins yield operating profit potential around $70 million.

If we factor in a 5% cost of debt on pro forma net debt and assume a statutory tax rate, I see net earnings potential at around $43 million. This would be equal to earnings of around $0.32 per share, indicating that expectations are high. Expectations have even more risen substantially as shares have risen to the $32 mark soon after the public offering, pushing up the enterprise valuation to around $4.5 billion.

Based on the revenue discussions, shares traded around 13 times sales and about a hundred times earnings, all rather demanding valuations. That however does not factor in about $16 million in annual amortization charges, as that could add about ten cents to earnings per share, but even in such a case, expectations remain very high.

Concluding Thoughts

The biggest risk in this story seems to be the valuation, as shares trade at rather demanding multiples here. Other risks include the reliance on a few large customers, as the current DOGE movement acts as a real potential threat to the business as well.

Other risks include that of technological developments, leaving the solutions provided by the business as ineffective, regulation on the industry, technological failures, as well as cybersecurity threats, as well as reliance on the US Defense budgets. That said, a funded backlog of $550 million as of September 2024 looks rather healthy, at over 1.5 times annual sales.

Nonetheless, the valuation argument is the largest risk in my view. All this makes me very cautious at current levels, as I am taking a wait-and-see approach to learn more about the business and its growth targets for 2025, before re-considering this neutral but cautionary stance.

data.title: MSD: Protected Against Trade Tensions, But Very Expensive
data.description:
The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund (NYSE:MSD) is a closed-end fund that investors can use as a way of getting a high level of current income along with exposure to the debt markets of various emerging market nations. There are relatively few of these funds around, although emerging market debt funds are somewhat more common than emerging market equity funds. This could be partially due to the fact that investing in emerging markets lost some of its appeal following the 1997 Asian financial crisis and the 2008 global financial crisis. After all, following the latter of these events, the United States began to deliver outsized returns as investment capital began to flow into this nation as something of a safe haven trade.

However, it is worth noting that emerging market bonds (EMB) have outperformed domestic investment-grade bonds (AGG) on a total return basis since January 2008:

We can see though that emerging market bonds still did worse than domestic junk bonds over the entire period. The majority of the underperformance came after the COVID-19 pandemic though, as we can see that emerging market bonds were actually beating domestic junk bonds on a total return basis until late 2021. This is at least partially due to the fact that emerging market bonds tend to have higher yields than many domestic investment-grade ones. We can see this by looking at the yields of the indices tracking emerging market bonds and domestic bonds:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index

5.14%

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index

3.74%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.55%

When it comes to bonds, higher-yielding assets are generally preferable to lower-yielding bonds for investors seeking to earn a high level of total return. This is especially true if you own enough bonds to diversify away the risk of defaults. Most closed-end funds and indices do this, so generally speaking, the higher-yielding assets will provide a better total return over the long term. This is exactly what we see in the chart above.

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund is able to provide an even higher yield than any of the above indices. As of the time of writing, this fund boasts a very impressive 13.27% yield, which compares very well to just about anything in the market. As we can see in this chart, the fund compares very well to its peers:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

13.27%

Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund (TEI)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

10.52%

Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund (EDF)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

14.17%

Western Asset Emerging Markets Debt Fund (EMD)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

10.52%

Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Domestic Debt (EDD)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

13.47%

As regular readers likely know, I do not ordinarily like to include two funds from the same fund house (in this case Morgan Stanley) when assembling a peer comparison. However, as already mentioned, there are only relatively few closed-end funds in existence that invest in the emerging markets debt sector, so the inclusion of both Morgan Stanley funds was necessary in order to have at least five funds in the chart.

As we can see, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund represents the median yield out of the peer group. The fund has a higher yield than two of its peers, including the popular Western Asset Emerging Markets Debt Fund, but it cannot compete with the very high-yielding Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund. This may discourage some investors from investing in this fund due to the fact that a higher level of income can be obtained by favoring some of its peers. However, as I pointed out in a recent article:

The market tends to value closed-end funds based on their yields. As such, when we see a fund with a yield that is substantially above that of its peers, it can be a sign that the market doubts the fund’s ability to sustain its payout.

This works in reverse as well, as a fund with a low yield when compared to its peers is generally viewed as having a sustainable distribution relative to the market.

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund obviously does not have an outsized yield relative to its peers. This suggests that the market does not harbor any doubts about the fund’s ability to sustain its distribution. That is something that should prove appealing to anyone who is seeking a safe and secure source of income. Of course, we should still have a look at the fund’s portfolio and finances in order to make our own judgment call about whether or not that is the case. Naturally, this will be one of the tasks that we focus on over the remainder of this article.

Performance Analysis

As regular readers may remember, we previously discussed the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund late in the summer of 2024. My previous article on this fund was published to Seeking Alpha on September 5, 2024, so approximately five months have passed since that time. A lot has happened in five months, but one of the major events that impacted the performance of emerging market securities has been the election of Donald Trump to his second term as U.S. president. Mr. Trump’s economic policies are rather protectionist in nature as one of his agendas is to increase manufacturing activity in the United States and provide companies with incentives to move their facilities back to the United States if they want to sell to U.S. consumers. This is very important because many emerging markets are hubs of manufacturing activity and export their products to the rest of the world. For example, China, Russia, Vietnam, and Brazil all have very large trade surpluses:

Most people would call those five emerging markets, and some people might include Saudi Arabia and Taiwan among the emerging market nations. The United States, on the other hand, traditionally runs a trade deficit with several of the above nations, including the emerging market ones. President Trump’s agenda thus could impact the economies of many emerging market nations, and if the balance of trade becomes more even, it will reduce the ability of exporting nations to obtain U.S. dollars. As we will see in this article, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund holds a lot of emerging market debt that is denominated in U.S. dollars, so if the ability of these countries to obtain U.S. dollars is reduced, it could put pressure on their ability to service their debts. Thus, an increasingly protectionist trade stance on the part of the United States increases the risk of investing in some of the securities that are held by this fund and the overall risks of the fund itself.

As I explained in a recent article, an improvement in the trade deficit that the United States runs with the rest of the world should strengthen the U.S. dollar against foreign currencies. This would actually cause foreign currency-denominated bonds to decrease when measured in terms of U.S. dollars. The effect here would likely be greater for emerging markets than for developed markets due to emerging markets being more dependent on exports to the United States as a source of economic strength and growth. However, there would not necessarily be an impact on the U.S. dollar-denominated debt of emerging markets (other than a possible price decrease due to rising risks as discussed in the previous paragraphs). The fund’s balance between U.S. dollar-denominated debt and foreign currency-denominated debt will thus have an impact on its performance as well.

The U.S. dollar started rising against many foreign currencies prior to the U.S. national election date. One reason for this is that the market began to suspect that Mr. Trump would win the election back in October and began to price the U.S. higher as an expected result of the policies that he was campaigning on. As we can see here, the U.S. dollar index (DXY) has risen by 5.49% since October 1, 2024:

As such, the fund would have started facing headwinds in early October, with the magnitude of these headwinds dependent on the amount of foreign debt that the fund holds.

Despite these headwinds, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund has held up pretty well since our last discussion. In fact, shares of the fund are up 3.90% since that article was published:

As we can see from the chart, this is significantly better than the 1.24% decline in the U.S. dollar-denominated emerging market bond index over the same period. This is admittedly somewhat confusing, given that this fund will be holding at least some of the same assets as the index. However, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund is actively managed, which can give it an advantage over an index fund in certain market conditions. After all, this fund’s management can generally pick and choose what bonds the fund purchases, as opposed to simply being beholden to purchasing whatever is in the index. That could account for some of the strong performance. Another potential factor could be the fund’s share price outperforming its underlying portfolio for whatever reason, which reduces the fund’s overall value proposition. At any rate, the substantial outperformance relative to the index suggests that we should have a closer look at the fund’s portfolio to see what exactly could be the cause of this outperformance. After all, we do not want to just jump in and buy an overpriced asset.

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund did not outperform the index solely on a share price basis. As I pointed out in the previous article on this fund:

A simple look at a closed-end fund’s share price performance does not necessarily provide an accurate picture of how investors in the fund did during a given period. This is because these funds tend to pay out all of their net investment profits to the shareholders, rather than relying on the capital appreciation of their share price to provide a return. This is the reason why the yields of these funds tend to be much higher than the yield of index funds or most other market assets.

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund paid out distributions to its investors over the five-month period. These distributions provided an additional return that is not accounted for in a simple chart of the fund’s share price performance. We should include them to see how investors in this fund really did, and when we do that we get this alternative chart:

As we can see, shareholders in the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund received a 10.53% total return over the approximately five-month period. This is much better than the 1.31% total return that investors in the index fund received, although including the distributions generally resulted in a better showing for both assets. The high total return provided by the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund seems likely to attract investors overall, and it is something that most of us will appreciate. After all, we all like to beat the market.

However, it is important to note that past performance is no guarantee of future results. As such, let us spend the remainder of this article looking at this fund in more depth and attempting to determine how it is likely to perform going forward.

About The Fund

According to the fund’s website, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund has the primary investment objective of providing its investors with a very high level of current income. This objective makes a great deal of sense when we consider the strategy that this fund employs, which is described in its most recent annual report:

The Fund seeks to achieve its investment objectives by investing, under normal circumstances, at least 80% of its managed assets in emerging markets domestic debt. To the extent that that Fund invests in derivative instruments that the Adviser believes have economic characteristics similar to debt securities of government and government-related issuers located in emerging market countries and of entities organized to restructure outstanding debts of such issuers, such investments will be counted for purposes of meeting the Fund’s investment objective.

An emerging country is any country that the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development or similar major financial institution has determined to have a low or middle economy or countries included in the Index.

The Adviser invests the Fund’s assets in emerging country debt securities that provide a high level of current income, while at the same time holding the potential for capital appreciation if the perceived creditworthiness of the issuer improves due to improving economic, financial, political, social or other conditions in the country in which the issuer is located. Currently, investing in many emerging country securities is not feasible or may involve unacceptable political risks.

As opportunities to invest in debt securities in other countries develop, the Fund expects to expand and further diversify the emerging countries in which it invests. While the Fund generally is not restricted in the portion of its assets which may be invested in a single country or region, it is intended that, under normal conditions, the Fund’s assets will be invested in issuers in at least three countries.

This description makes it very clear that the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund invests in debt securities issued by countries in emerging markets. This is exactly what we would expect from the fund’s name, as well as its fixed-income classification. As I stated in a previous article:

Bonds by their very nature are income securities, as they do not deliver any net capital gains over their lifetimes. This makes sense, as an investor will purchase a bond at face value and receive face value back when the bond matures. The only investment return for a bond held over its entire lifetime is the coupon payments made to the bond’s owner. Thus, bonds do not deliver capital appreciation over their lifetimes.

As a result of this, any bond fund should have a current income objective. After all, most bonds are not capable of delivering any net capital gains. There is one exception to this, though, and that is that foreign currency-denominated bonds might be able to deliver net capital gains when measured in U.S. dollars. This happens when the U.S. dollar declines against whatever currency the bond is denominated in because the face value will, when the bond matures, convert into more U.S. dollars than the investor needed to use to obtain the bond in the first place. The same thing is true for any bond that is denominated in a currency other than the investor’s own currency. This does not mean that the bond actually appreciates in value, though, as the bond itself does not deliver a net gain when measured in terms of its own currency denomination. It still might be important to the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund though, since this fund evaluates its performance in U.S. dollar terms.

As the above description states, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund invests primarily in debt securities. The fund’s third-quarter 2024 holdings report provides the following asset allocation as of September 30, 2024:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Fixed-Income Securities

96.4%

Warrants

0.8%

Money Market Funds

4.3%

U.S. Treasury Bills

0.2%

As we can see, the overwhelming majority of the fund’s assets are invested in fixed-income securities, which is pretty much what we expected to see. Curiously, though, the fund’s allocation to fixed-income securities is down somewhat from the figures that were provided in its semi-annual report, which we saw in the previous article. Please recall that in the previous article, we saw this asset allocation chart for June 30, 2024:

Thus, we see the following changes over the June 30, 2024, through September 30, 2024, period:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets as of June 30, 2024

% of Net Assets as of September 30, 2024

% Change

Fixed-Income Securities

98.1%

96.4%

-1.7%

Warrants

0.0%

0.8%

+0.8%

Money Market Fund

1.4%

4.3%

+2.9%

U.S. Treasury Bills

0.2%

0.2%

No Change

The decrease in the fund’s fixed-income securities allocation in exchange for an increase in its money market holdings is rather interesting. This could suggest a risk-off trade, which, unfortunately, appears to have cost the fund a certain amount of return. As we can see here, the emerging markets bond index increased by 6.64% over the June 30, 2024 through September 30, 2024 period:

We can see that emerging markets bonds not only delivered a very positive return over the period, but they actually managed to outperform the S&P 500 Index (SP500) over the period. It is, to put it mildly, very rare for any bond index to outperform common stocks.

As some readers might remember, though, the domestic bond market was also fairly strong over the period in question. During the months of July, August, and the first half of September 2024, investors were very aggressively purchasing domestic bonds in anticipation of the Federal Reserve’s projected September federal funds reduction. However, domestic bonds did not manage to outperform the S&P 500 Index as investment-grade bonds were only up 5.22% and junk bonds were only up 4.42% over the same period:

Thus, the fund appears to have cost itself some potential returns by selling off some of its emerging market bonds in exchange for cash over the period. We do not, however, know exactly when the fund made this trade, so we cannot determine the exact magnitude of the sacrificed gains. The closer that the fund made this trade to mid-September (the emerging markets bond index peaked on September 19), the more returns the fund gave up.

Earlier in this article, I stated that a protectionist trade policy on behalf of the United States might make it more difficult for emerging market nations to obtain the U.S. dollars that they require to service their debt. The chart may be slightly confusing, as a negative value in the balance of trade column indicates that the country has a positive balance of trade with the United States. In other words, the United States has a trade deficit with the country in question. It would, at least in theory, be those countries that would be most at risk should protectionist trade policies make it more difficult for them to obtain the U.S. dollars that are needed to service their international debts. As we can see though, the fund appears to have done a pretty good job diversifying its country risk. The only three countries that account for more than 3% of the fund’s net assets and have trade surpluses with the United States are Ecuador, Ghana, and Mexico. None of these countries has more than a 5% weighting in the fund, so none of them account for a particularly large proportion of the fund’s assets.

The exact measures that the new U.S. administration will take remain a point of uncertainty. Indeed, Rabobank pointed out yesterday that the Administration appears to be willing to negotiate with other countries in respect to trade policies. As such, it remains difficult to determine our exact risks here, but the fund’s lack of significant exposure to any individual country is comforting.

Valuation

Shares of the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund are currently trading at a 2.57% premium on net asset value. This is a slightly better price than the 3.89% premium that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month, but it does still represent a premium to the value of the underlying portfolio. As such, anyone purchasing shares of the fund today is paying more for the underlying assets than they are actually worth.

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund is currently looking very expensive historically. Over the past three years, the fund has traded at prices ranging from a 16.12% discount to a 4.56% premium on net asset value. The average price over the period was a 9.26% discount on net asset value. That is a substantially better price than the current one, which suggests that this fund is very expensive compared to its long-term average.

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund is also trading at a very high price compared to its peers. We can see that in this comparison chart:

As such, the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund is quite expensive when compared to its peers. Admittedly, this is perhaps the best-run fund out of this group, but its current price certainly looks far too expensive.

The best move here is probably to wait until this fund begins trading at a discount before purchasing its shares.

Expenses

The Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund has an expense ratio of 1.26%, which will likely seem high to any investor who is used to the sub-1% ratios of most index funds. However, this fund is not especially expensive when compared to its peers:
